title: Wealth Should Buy Health. Why Doesn't It?
dek: Across 217 countries, income predicts a lot. The remaining gap is where institutions matter most.

beats.label: Beat 1
beats.headline: Income predicts a lot, not everything
beats.body: The line is the benchmark. The distance to the line is the story.
beats.aria_summary: Global scatter introduces expectation line and residual distance.

cards.headline: b1_c1
cards.body:
Before any country appears, start with the pattern.

Richer countries usually live longer. That is one of the most stable patterns in global development data.

cards.headline: b1_c2
cards.body: If this were a perfect world, every dot would sit on a smooth curve: one level of income, one expected level of survival. But real countries do not line up that neatly.

cards.headline: b1_c3
cards.body:
In 2010, income explains about two-thirds of cross-country variation in life expectancy in this dataset. That is substantial. It is also incomplete.

That remaining third is where countries with similar income end up with very different life chances.

cards.headline: b1_c4
cards.body:
Treat the line as a benchmark, not a verdict. The key quantity is the vertical distance between a country and that line.

[[Above the line]] means people are living longer than income alone predicts. [[Below the line]] means they are living shorter lives than income alone predicts.

cards.headline: b1_c5
cards.body:
A typical country is not exactly on the line. In 2010, the median absolute life-expectancy gap is about 2.7 years. So modest divergence is normal.

But the tails are not modest. One in ten countries sits more than about 7.8 years away from expectation. At the extremes, the spread is wider still: around +11 years on the high side and about -18 years on the low side.

cards.headline: b1_c6
cards.body:
Here is the premise: [[income is powerful]], [[income is not destiny]], and the gap is big enough to matter in real lives.

Once that frame is clear, we can track countries the way this chart tracks them: not by rank alone, but by distance from expectation and movement over time.

beats.label: Beat 2
beats.headline: Rwanda bends the trajectory
beats.body: The reveal is time-based first, model-based second.
beats.aria_summary: Rwanda under-5 reveal and trajectory crossing above expectation.

cards.headline: b2_c3
cards.body:
That drop is the first fact.

The second fact is where this period starts. Rwanda enters the 2006-2015 window after a decade of state and health-system reconstruction following the mid-1990s collapse.

cards.headline: b2_c4
cards.body:
The baseline is not a mature, fully resourced system. It is a country rebuilding capacity while still carrying severe mortality burden.

So the movement in this decade is not just "improvement happened." It is improvement happening from a difficult starting point, and happening across multiple frontline outcomes at once.

cards.headline: b2_c5
cards.body: In this dataset: maternal mortality drops from 534 to 290, infant mortality drops from 62.6 to 31.4, under-5 mortality drops from 98.3 to 42.0, and life expectancy rises from 57.2 to 66.7.

cards.headline: b2_c6
cards.body:
More important than one endpoint is the trajectory relative to expectation. Rwanda starts slightly below the income-based line and moves above it by 2010, then farther above by 2015.

That does not prove one policy caused one outcome.

cards.headline: b2_c7
cards.body:
It does show the gap can move in the favorable direction for a sustained stretch, not only in a one-year spike.

And it shows what [[institutions matter]] looks like in practice: the same income level can deliver different survival odds depending on how care is financed, organized, staffed, and reached by households.

beats.label: Beat 3
beats.headline: A second country confirms the pattern
beats.body: Rwanda is not a one-case narrative.
beats.aria_summary: Vietnam and Rwanda highlighted above the same expectation line.

cards.headline: b3_c1
cards.body: Rwanda is not the only dot on that side.

cards.headline: b3_c2
cards.body:
In 2010, Vietnam sits roughly 11 years above its income-based life-expectancy expectation.

Vietnam comes from a different regional and institutional path, but the signal is the same: [[above the line]].

cards.headline: b3_c3
cards.body:
Vietnam is also not a one-year outlier in this series. It starts the decade far above the line and remains far above it by 2015.

That brief confirmation matters because it lowers the chance that Rwanda is a one-case narrative.

beats.label: Beat 4
beats.headline: The richest country still sits below the line
beats.body: Money and delivery are different things.
beats.aria_summary: U.S. below-line position, decade persistence, and peer comparison cues.

cards.headline: b4_c1
cards.body: Now shift to a country that should, on wealth alone, be difficult to place below the line.

cards.headline: b4_c2
cards.body: In 2010, the United States records life expectancy around 78.5 years, while the income-based expectation is about 81.1.

cards.headline: b4_c3
cards.body:
The point is not that the U.S. is low-income and constrained. It is one of the richest countries in the dataset period.

And within a tight band of similarly wealthy countries, the U.S. sits near the bottom on life expectancy.

cards.headline: b4_c4
cards.body:
If this were only a one-year anomaly, the gap would quickly close.

It does not.

cards.headline: b4_c5
cards.body:
The U.S. life-expectancy distance from expectation is below the line in 2006, still below in 2010, and farther below again by 2015.

That gives us a decade pattern, not a snapshot.

cards.headline: b4_c6
cards.body:
The same pattern appears in child and maternal signals.

U.S. maternal mortality is flat at 14.0 in 2006 and 14.0 in 2015, while high-income peers decline on average.

cards.headline: b4_c7
cards.body:
U.S. infant mortality improves over the decade, but by less than the average high-income peer reduction. U.S. under-5 mortality also improves, but again more slowly than peer averages.

One concrete comparator: Canada moves maternal mortality from 9.0 to 7.0 over the same period.

cards.headline: b4_c8
cards.body:
None of this means income is irrelevant. The U.S. still has resources most countries do not.

It means [[money and delivery are different things]]. A country can spend heavily while the care system remains fragmented. Coverage can expand while access still varies by geography, cost, and continuity.

In this chart language, that complexity shows up as one geometric fact: the dot stays below the line.

beats.label: Beat 5
beats.headline: The negative tail is structural
beats.aria_summary: Equatorial Guinea deep underperformance and Sierra Leone maternal floor marker.

cards.headline: b5_c1
cards.body: At the opposite end of the distribution, Equatorial Guinea shows how large the negative distance can become.

cards.headline: b5_c2
cards.body:
In 2010, life expectancy is about 55.9 years against an income-based expectation near 73.7.

That is not a small miss. It is a structural miss. It is the kind of distance no income ranking can justify on its own.

cards.headline: b5_c3
cards.body:
Supporting indicators move in the same direction: infant and under-5 mortality are far worse than income-based expectation in the same year.

Then the floor appears.

cards.headline: b5_c4
cards.body:
Sierra Leone records maternal mortality of 1,630 per 100,000 in 2010. That marks the floor of this distribution in 2010.

Different country profile. Different income path. [[Same failure.]]

beats.label: Beat 6
beats.headline: Compression happens, divergence persists
beats.body: The spread tightens, but does not collapse.
beats.aria_summary: Global year scrubber with distribution strip compression over 2006 to 2015.

cards.headline: b6_c1
cards.body: So what moves these gaps?

cards.headline: b6_c2
cards.body: From 2006 to 2015, global distance from the line compresses somewhat: median absolute gap moves from about 3.1 years to about 2.7, 90th-percentile distance drops from about 9.3 to about 7.3, and mean absolute distance also declines.

cards.headline: b6_c3
cards.body:
So the world in this dataset gets somewhat less dispersed relative to income expectation.

But that compression is partial, not transformational.

cards.headline: b6_c4
cards.body: Large positive and negative divergences remain present in every year. The distribution does not collapse around zero.

cards.headline: b6_c5
cards.body: This is not an abstract prompt. In this data, upward movers usually do not improve in only one place. They improve across connected survival pathways: maternal care, infant outcomes, under-5 outcomes, and life expectancy moving together rather than in isolation.

cards.headline: b6_c6
cards.body:
That pattern suggests system-wide improvement, not a single isolated intervention.

A useful way to read the chart is to ask three operational questions.

cards.headline: b6_c7
cards.body:
First, can people enter care early and reliably through primary care rather than only at crisis point?

Second, when higher-acuity care is needed, do referral chains actually connect local facilities to district and hospital capacity in time?

Third, does financing protect continuity, or do cost shocks force households to delay care until conditions are harder and more expensive to treat?

cards.headline: b6_c8
cards.body:
Those are not ideological questions. They are system design questions, and they are measurable in outcomes over time.

Rwanda's movement above expectation is accompanied by broad frontline improvement. The U.S. remains below expectation over a decade despite high aggregate resources.

cards.headline: b6_c9
cards.body:
Equatorial Guinea sits far below expectation, and Sierra Leone shows what the floor can look like when care systems fail.

None of this yields a universal recipe that copies cleanly from one country to another. But it does narrow where to look: conversion capacity, referral reliability, and continuity in maternal-child pathways are where gains are most likely to compound.

cards.headline: b6_c10
cards.body:
That persistence is the governance burden: which arrangements move dots upward, which arrangements stall despite resources, and which reforms reduce the deadliest outcomes at the bottom.

You can watch that burden accumulate or ease, year by year, on the same line.

beats.label: Beat 7
beats.headline: Progress is governable
beats.body: What happens next is a governance question, not a math question.
beats.aria_summary: Final global settle with constrained-hope close.

cards.headline: b7_c1
cards.body: The hopeful reading is not that progress is automatic. It is that progress is governable.

cards.headline: b7_c2
cards.body: What happens next is a [[governance question]], not a math question.

bullets: Scope: 217 countries, 2006-2015, using transformed MDG-aligned data in website/data/.
Frame: country-year outcomes are compared to income-based expectations using life expectancy as the persistent benchmark.
This is descriptive, not causal. Residual gaps identify over- and under-performance but do not isolate mechanisms.
Country averages hide within-country inequality and measurement limits.
External context citations are documented in docs/story_context_endnotes_v5.md.
